feedback.text: I really like this initiative, but it's missing a couple things related to software. A lot of creative software, video games and even hardware are currently at risk of being lost, or have already been lost by consumers because of publishers deactivating services once they no longer see value in supporting them. Some examples being the Spotify Car Thing, Ubisoft's The Crew (which has also launched a citizens initiative campaign which has currently accumulated almost 1,5 million signatures at the time of writing, being focussed purely on the video game side of this issue) and Autodesk SoftImage. Since a lot of companies now use liscences for their software instead of buying the software outright under the guise of being able to keep pushing out new updates, we as consumers lose the ability to keep using the digital products we pay for. When a company decides to retire a product, it becomes completely unusable within the timespan of usually no more than a year. Technology ofcourse keeps moving in this day and age, but we as consumers should have the right to keep using the things we pay for. Even when a company decides to want to focus on different things, we should be able to retain ownership over what we have spent our hard earned money on. Companies should not be forced to keep supporting a product, but it should always remain accessible. Once we pay for a product, it should be ours to use however we please within legal limits and should be able to use it until we lose the ability due to technology around us moving to the point where a program could no longer run on a modern computer rather than when a company says so. And even when that happens, we should be able to run it on a different computer with era appropriate hardware. Ownership of digital products is, and has been under threat for years now and it should be an important part of the Digital Fairness Act.
